reflection:
Across these games, the “verify once, then be ready to volunteer” strategy worked consistently well. Spending exactly one coin in Round 1 to verify Q2 gave me at least one answer I personally trusted and reliably produced redundancy on Q2 (and indirectly on Q4 through others’ behavior). This small, predictable investment improved confidence without draining coins.

The key refinement that worked was treating Round 2 as the default volunteering moment once two conditions were met: (1) some questions had multi-endorsed capsules (usually Q2 and Q4), and (2) there were no contradictions anywhere. Volunteering at that “good enough” point repeatedly led to correct passwords and survival with most of my coins intact.

What didn’t work in earlier games was waiting for extra verification beyond that threshold. Additional checks rarely uncovered new information but always required another round, which introduced more random-death risk if no one volunteered. The games also reinforced that even a good strategy can’t eliminate variance: I could still be the randomly eliminated player despite following a solid policy.

Mechanically, I learned that verification has sharply diminishing returns. Its value peaks once a couple of questions are double-checked and no conflicts appear; after that, further verification mostly just trades survival odds for marginal (and often unnecessary) certainty.

Next time, I will stick to:
- One low-cost verification in Round 1 to seed redundancy.
- Volunteering in Round 2 whenever there are no conflicts and at least two multi-endorsed questions, even if others remain single-endorsed.
- Treating every extra round as a real survival cost, avoiding perfectionism when the information is already “good enough.”

The “early verification” strategy worked consistently well. In each game, I spent 1 coin in Round 1 to verify Q2, turning it from a single-source answer into a double-endorsed anchor (Q2-B+A). Because my own puzzle was always simple and already sealed, this was a high‑value move: I traded a small personal cost for a large increase in shared reliability. When the game reached Round 2 and I was still alive, a second verification on the weakest link (typically Q3 with only one endorsement) further reduced risk at low additional cost.

What did not work was collective hesitation around volunteering. By Round 2, we often had: my trusted answer for Q1, a double-endorsed Q2, and Q4 effectively double-covered. At that point, Q3 was sometimes the only single-endorsed capsule, and further verification had sharply diminishing returns. Nonetheless, no one volunteered immediately in several cases, causing random eliminations despite already-strong information.

From these runs, I learned to treat coins purely as tools for buying information, not as something to hoard. One or two targeted verifications dramatically improve the safety of the final password, but beyond that, more checks don’t meaningfully change the odds while the background risk of random death remains.

Next time, I would keep the same verification pattern—spend 1 coin early on Q2, then verify the weakest remaining question—but adjust my volunteering behavior. As soon as every surviving puzzle is either double-endorsed or as covered as it can possibly be given the deaths so far, I should volunteer in that same round. Accepting small residual uncertainty is better than risking another full round with the threat of random elimination.

What worked / didn’t work  
My core strategy was stable and effective: trust my own solved puzzle, spend 1 coin in Round 1 to verify Q4, then be ready to volunteer once every puzzle was either double-endorsed or uncontested. Verifying Q4 consistently worked: it gave the “last piece” a second endorsement, so when D died randomly, the group still had a trusted final digit. What did not work was collective over-caution in Round 1: when nobody volunteered, the random-death mechanic repeatedly killed a player with no strategic benefit, sometimes even me.

What I learned about the mechanics  
- Moving a puzzle from 1 endorsement to 2 is high-value; extra verifications beyond that are usually low-impact.  
- Random death for “no volunteer” is a huge risk: failing to act can be worse than making a well-reasoned but slightly uncertain attempt.  
- Once each puzzle is either (a) double-endorsed or (b) single and uncontested, the marginal value of more checks is smaller than the danger of another no-volunteer round.  
- Coins are best viewed as a way to buy *sufficient* reliability, not as a score to hoard.

What I’ll do differently next time  
I’ll keep the Round 1 verify-Q4 opener but enforce a strict pivot: as soon as every puzzle is double-endorsed or uncontested, I should stop verifying and be willing to volunteer. I’ll also mentally accept that some uncertainty is optimal; aiming for perfect safety only feeds the random-death mechanic.
